---
role: proof
locale: en
of_concept: ascending-chain-finitely-generated
source_book: gtm030
source_chapter: "Chapter VI"
source_section: "4. The chain conditions"
---

**Proof.** We first assume that the ascending chain condition holds and let $\mathfrak{N}$ be any submodule of $\mathfrak{M}$. If $\mathfrak{N} = 0$, then $\mathfrak{N}$ is generated by $0$. If $\mathfrak{N} \neq 0$, let $u_1$ be any non-zero element of $\mathfrak{N}$ and let $(u_1)$ denote the submodule generated by $u_1$. If $(u_1) = \mathfrak{N}$, we are done. Otherwise, pick $u_2 \in \mathfrak{N} \setminus (u_1)$. Then the submodule $(u_1, u_2)$ generated by $u_1, u_2$ properly contains $(u_1)$. If $(u_1, u_2) = \mathfrak{N}$, we are done. Otherwise, continuing in this manner we obtain a strictly increasing chain
$$(u_1) \subsetneq (u_1, u_2) \subsetneq (u_1, u_2, u_3) \subsetneq \cdots$$
By the ascending chain condition, this chain must terminate after finitely many steps, say at step $r$. Then $(u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_r) = \mathfrak{N}$, so $\mathfrak{N}$ is finitely generated.

Conversely, assume that every submodule of $\mathfrak{M}$ is finitely generated. Let
$$\mathfrak{N}_1 \subseteq \mathfrak{N}_2 \subseteq \mathfrak{N}_3 \subseteq \cdots$$
be an increasing sequence of submodules. Set $\mathfrak{N} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} \mathfrak{N}_i$. It is easy to verify that $\mathfrak{N}$ is a submodule of $\mathfrak{M}$. By hypothesis, $\mathfrak{N}$ is finitely generated, say $\mathfrak{N} = (v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_m)$. Each generator $v_j$ lies in some $\mathfrak{N}_{i_j}$. Let $r = \max\{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_m\}$. Then all generators $v_1, \ldots, v_m$ belong to $\mathfrak{N}_r$, so $\mathfrak{N} \subseteq \mathfrak{N}_r$. Since $\mathfrak{N}_r \subseteq \mathfrak{N}$ by construction, we have $\mathfrak{N}_r = \mathfrak{N}$. Consequently $\mathfrak{N}_r = \mathfrak{N}_{r+1} = \mathfrak{N}_{r+2} = \cdots$, and the ascending chain condition holds.
